---
title: "Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth"
date: "2026-07-03"
author: "Prof. Shin"
categories:
  - Data Science
  - Artificial Intelligence
  - Time Series
image: "thumbnail.jpg"
description: |
  When a series' mean and variance drift over time, a model trained on the past
  meets a test set it has never seen the scale of. This article explains
  non-stationarity and concept drift from the ground up and builds — with runnable
  code — from naive normalization to Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN).
lang: en
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: false
code-fold: false
code-tools: false
bibliography: ../../references.bib
jupyter: nn_torch
execute:
  echo: true
  output: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
format:
  html:
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
---

## 1. The problem: a model that trained on one world and is tested in another

Most machine learning rests on a quiet assumption: the data you train on and the
data you predict on are drawn from the same distribution. Time series routinely
break that assumption. A series is **stationary** if its statistical
properties — mean, variance, autocorrelation — stay constant over time. Real
series are usually **non-stationary**: sales grow, prices inflate, sensors drift,
a product goes viral and the level jumps. The statistics you measured on the
training window no longer describe the window you are forecasting.

This has a specific, damaging consequence for forecasting, and it is worth
stating precisely before writing any model. If a model learns a mapping from an
input window to a future window, and the *scale* of those windows keeps rising,
then at test time the model receives inputs numerically unlike anything in
training. It is being asked to extrapolate, not interpolate. Let us make the
phenomenon concrete with a synthetic series that has all three troubles at once:
an exponential trend, abrupt level shifts (a stand-in for concept drift), and
noise whose size grows with the level (heteroscedasticity).

```{python}
#| label: make-series
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

def make_series(N=2400):
    """Synthetic non-stationary series with three sources of drift:
    - exp(0.0028 t): exponential trend (multiplicative growth)
    - level shifts at t=600,1200,1800: abrupt regime changes (concept drift)
    - noise scaled by sqrt(trend): heteroscedastic (variance grows over time)"""
    t = np.arange(N)
    trend = np.exp(0.0028 * t)
    season = 0.4 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 48) * np.sqrt(trend)
    level = np.zeros(N)
    for cp in [600, 1200, 1800]:
        level[cp:] += np.random.uniform(2, 5) * np.sqrt(trend[cp])
    noise = 0.08 * np.random.randn(N) * trend
    return (trend + season + level + noise).astype(np.float32)

series = make_series()

# Quantify the non-stationarity: statistics per third of the series.
for i, part in enumerate(np.array_split(series, 3)):
    print(f"third {i+1}: mean={part.mean():8.2f}  std={part.std():7.2f}")
```

The three thirds of the series have wildly different means and standard
deviations — the mean grows by more than an order of magnitude from the first
third to the last. A model that sees only the early portion during training has
no way to know the later portion will live at a completely different scale. This
is the failure that normalization must address.

## 2. Naming the two failures: distribution shift and concept drift

Two terms describe what is going wrong, and keeping them distinct helps you
choose a fix.

**Distribution shift** is the umbrella term: the joint distribution of the data
changes between training and deployment. In time series this shows up most
visibly as a change in the marginal statistics — the mean and variance of the
values themselves move over time [@kim2022revin]. The exponential trend in the
code above is pure distribution shift: nothing about the *relationship* between
past and future changed, only the scale.

**Concept drift** is the sharper, and more dangerous, cousin. Here the
relationship between inputs and target — the underlying function you are trying
to learn — changes over time. A level shift caused by a new competitor entering
the market, a regulation, or a pandemic is concept drift: the rule that generated
the data before the change no longer generates it after. Recent work formalizes
that in forecasting this drift has two distinct faces: it can shift *within* the
space of input windows over time (intra-space shift), and it can differ *between*
the input window and the horizon window you are predicting (inter-space shift)
[@fan2023dishts]. The second is easy to overlook and is exactly what makes
forecasting harder than ordinary supervised learning: even a single training
example spans two possibly-different distributions, its lookback and its horizon.

Normalization is the first line of defense against the distribution-shift part.
The rest of this article builds up the normalization toolkit in order of
sophistication, measuring each step.

## 3. First attempt: global normalization, and why it fails

The reflex from tabular ML is to standardize: subtract the mean, divide by the
standard deviation, computed once over the training set. This *global*
normalization stabilizes the numeric range for optimization, but it does nothing
about drift, because a single fixed mean and variance cannot describe a series
whose mean and variance are moving targets. Subtracting the training mean from a
test window that lives ten times higher leaves a huge residual the model was
never trained on.

We need the supervised machinery in place to measure this, so let us set up
windowing (respecting time order — no shuffling, no leakage) and a small MLP
forecaster.

```{python}
#| label: setup-model
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
torch.manual_seed(0)

L, H = 48, 24    # lookback length, forecast horizon

def make_windows(x, L, H):
    """Turn a 1-D series into (X, Y): X is (n, L) lookback, Y is (n, H) horizon.
    Windows are built in time order; window i never contains future information."""
    X, Y = [], []
    for i in range(L, len(x) - H + 1):
        X.append(x[i - L:i])
        Y.append(x[i:i + H])
    return np.stack(X), np.stack(Y)

X, Y = make_windows(series, L, H)
n = len(X)
split = int(n * 0.7)                       # first 70% train, last 30% test (chronological)
Xtr, Ytr, Xte, Yte = X[:split], Y[:split], X[split:], Y[split:]

class MLP(nn.Module):
    """A minimal forecaster: L inputs -> 128 hidden -> H outputs."""
    def __init__(self, L, H):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(L, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(128, H))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

print(f"train windows: {Xtr.shape}, test windows: {Xte.shape}")
print(f"train input mean: {Xtr.mean():.1f}, test input mean: {Xte.mean():.1f} "
      f"({Xte.mean()/Xtr.mean():.1f}x higher)")
```

The last line is the whole problem in one number: the test windows sit at roughly
an order of magnitude higher level than the training windows. Any model, and any
*global* normalization fit on the training set, is now extrapolating.

## 4. The idea that works: normalize each instance, then put it back

The key move, introduced as **Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN)**, is to
stop normalizing globally and start normalizing *per instance* — and, crucially,
to make the normalization reversible [@kim2022revin]. For each input window, you
remove that window's own mean and standard deviation before the model sees it, so
every window arrives at the model looking stationary regardless of what absolute
level it came from. Then, after the model produces its (normalized) forecast, you
add the window's statistics back to return the prediction to the original scale.

Formally, for an input window $x \in \mathbb{R}^{L}$ (one channel), RevIN computes
the instance statistics

$$
\mu_x = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{t=1}^{L} x_t, \qquad
\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{t=1}^{L}(x_t - \mu_x)^2,
$$ {#eq-stats}

normalizes with a learnable affine transform (parameters $\gamma, \beta$),

$$
\hat{x}_t = \gamma \, \frac{x_t - \mu_x}{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta,
$$ {#eq-norm}

feeds $\hat{x}$ through the model to get a normalized forecast $\hat{y}$, and then
**denormalizes** by inverting @eq-norm with the *same* $\mu_x, \sigma_x$:

$$
y_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \epsilon}\;\cdot\;\frac{\hat{y}_t - \beta}{\gamma} + \mu_x.
$$ {#eq-denorm}

Here $\epsilon$ is a small constant for numerical stability, and $\gamma, \beta
\in \mathbb{R}$ are learned. The symmetry is the point: the non-stationary
information (the level and scale of each window) is *removed* at the input and
*restored* at the output, so the model only ever has to learn the stationary
shape, not the moving level [@kim2022revin].

Before measuring accuracy, it is worth seeing directly what the normalization
does to the input distribution. This is the conceptual heart of the method:

```{python}
#| label: revin-effect
def windows_only(x, L):
    return np.stack([x[i - L:i] for i in range(L, len(x) - L)])

W = windows_only(series, L)
raw_means = W.mean(axis=1)
raw_stds = W.std(axis=1)

# Per-instance normalization (RevIN's removal step), no affine:
Wn = (W - W.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)) / (W.std(axis=1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8)

print("RAW windows    : mean in [%.1f, %.1f], std in [%.1f, %.1f]"
      % (raw_means.min(), raw_means.max(), raw_stds.min(), raw_stds.max()))
print("RevIN-normed   : mean in [%.3f, %.3f], std in [%.3f, %.3f]"
      % (Wn.mean(axis=1).min(), Wn.mean(axis=1).max(),
         Wn.std(axis=1).min(), Wn.std(axis=1).max()))
print("\nWindow means spanned a %.0fx range; RevIN collapses them to ~0."
      % (raw_means.max() / raw_means.min()))
```

The raw window means span a range of several hundred to one; after per-instance
normalization every window has mean ~0 and standard deviation ~1. From the
model's point of view the drifting series has been turned into a stationary one.
That is what lets a model trained on the low-level early data still work on the
high-level late data — the level was never the model's problem to solve.

## 5. Measuring it: three strategies head to head

Now we train the same MLP three ways — no normalization, global normalization,
and RevIN-style per-instance normalization with denormalization — and compare.
The training loop is deliberately explicit so each strategy's normalization is
visible.

```{python}
#| label: train-compare
def rmse(a, b):
    return float(np.sqrt(((a - b) ** 2).mean()))

def run(mode, epochs=120):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    Xt, Yt, Xv = torch.tensor(Xtr), torch.tensor(Ytr), torch.tensor(Xte)
    if mode == "global":
        m, sd = Xt.mean(), Xt.std()
    model = MLP(L, H)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    lossf = nn.MSELoss()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        model.train()
        if mode == "revin":
            mu = Xt.mean(1, keepdim=True); sg = Xt.std(1, keepdim=True) + 1e-5
            xin, ytar = (Xt - mu) / sg, (Yt - mu) / sg   # normalize input AND target per instance
        elif mode == "global":
            xin, ytar = (Xt - m) / sd, (Yt - m) / sd
        else:  # none
            xin, ytar = Xt, Yt
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss = lossf(model(xin), ytar)
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        if mode == "revin":
            mu = Xv.mean(1, keepdim=True); sg = Xv.std(1, keepdim=True) + 1e-5
            pred = (model((Xv - mu) / sg) * sg + mu).numpy()   # denormalize back to scale
        elif mode == "global":
            pred = (model((Xv - m) / sd) * sd + m).numpy()
        else:
            pred = model(Xv).numpy()
    return pred

preds = {mode: run(mode) for mode in ["none", "global", "revin"]}
print("absolute test RMSE (dominated by the large-scale test region):")
for mode, p in preds.items():
    print(f"  {mode:8s} {rmse(p, Yte):8.3f}")
```

The absolute RMSE numbers are close, and that itself is a lesson worth reading
carefully rather than glossing over. Two things are happening. First, absolute
RMSE on this test set is dominated by the handful of very-high-scale windows at
the end, so it is a poor lens for comparing strategies — a large error on a
large-scale window swamps everything else. Second, a plain MLP with a ReLU layer
can itself learn a surprising amount of scaling, so on smooth synthetic drift the
gap to "no normalization" is small. To compare fairly we need a **scale-free
metric**: divide each window's error by that window's own level, so a 5% error
counts the same whether the window sits at 10 or at 500.

```{python}
#| label: scale-free
def relative_rmse(pred, true, Xwin):
    """Per-window RMSE divided by the window's mean level (a MASE-like, scale-free score)."""
    scale = np.abs(Xwin).mean(axis=1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8
    per_window = np.sqrt(((pred - true) ** 2).mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)) / scale
    return float(per_window.mean())

print("scale-free relative RMSE (lower is better):")
scores = {}
for mode, p in preds.items():
    scores[mode] = relative_rmse(p, Yte, Xte)
    print(f"  {mode:8s} {scores[mode]:.4f}")

print(f"\nRevIN vs global normalization: "
      f"{(scores['global'] - scores['revin']) / scores['global'] * 100:+.1f}%")
```

On the scale-free metric RevIN edges out global normalization by a few percent,
while global normalization is actually the worst of the three here — subtracting
a single fixed mean from windows that live far above it is worse than doing
nothing. This modest, honest gap is characteristic: on clean synthetic data with
a cooperative model the effect is real but small. The published case for RevIN is
built on real datasets and stronger backbones, where removing per-instance level
before a Transformer or linear model yields substantial and consistent gains
across horizons [@kim2022revin]; a simple linear model wrapped in RevIN has been
reported to match or beat far heavier models on standard benchmarks.

## 6. Knowing the limits: when removing the level removes the signal

RevIN is not free, and the reason is conceptually important. By stripping each
window's mean and variance, you are asserting that the level and scale are
*nuisance* — information to be removed and restored, not used. Sometimes that
assertion is wrong. If a rising level or a change in variance is itself
predictive of the future — if the trend persists into the horizon in a way the
stationary shape cannot capture — then normalizing it away discards signal.

This is the critique behind the **Non-stationary Transformer**, which observes
that naive stationarization can *over-stationarize*: it can erase precisely the
non-stationary events the model needs to distinguish, so the authors pair
stationarization with a "de-stationary attention" that re-injects the removed
statistics back into the model's internals rather than only at the output
[@liu2022nonstationary]. It is also the motivation for **Dish-TS**, which points
out that RevIN implicitly assumes the input window and the horizon window share
statistics — that the mean you remove from the lookback is the right mean to
restore on the horizon. When the input and output spaces have genuinely different
distributions (inter-space shift), a single set of statistics is not enough, and
Dish-TS learns separate distribution coefficients for the input and output spaces
[@fan2023dishts].

The practical reading: instance normalization is a strong, cheap default that
fixes the common case (drifting level), but it encodes an assumption — level and
scale are nuisance, and lookback statistics predict horizon statistics — that you
should check against your data rather than accept blindly.

## 7. Conclusion

Non-stationarity is not a nuisance to be denoised away at the edges; it is the
central obstacle between a model that fit the past and a model that predicts the
future. The progression in this article is the progression the field itself took.
Global normalization stabilizes optimization but cannot track a moving mean, and
on strongly drifting data it can underperform doing nothing at all. Per-instance
reversible normalization — remove each window's level and scale, predict the
stationary shape, then restore the level — is the move that actually addresses
drift, and it is cheap enough to wrap around any model. Its own limitation points
to the frontier: when the level *is* the signal, or when the lookback and horizon
distributions genuinely differ, you need methods that re-inject or separately
model those statistics rather than simply removing them. Measure your series'
drift first, with a scale-free metric, and let the size and nature of that drift
decide how much normalization machinery you actually need.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
